content.text: Language models generate text one token at a time. At each step the model produces a vector of real-valued scores —
— one for every token in the vocabulary.

is an inference-time parameter that adds a fixed numerical offset to any subset of those logits before the softmax step, directly shifting the probability distribution without changing model weights or requiring fine-tuning. It is one of the most precise low-level controls available in inference APIs, and understanding exactly where and how it acts is essential for any developer building agentic systems that need controlled, structured output.
After the transformer's final layer produces raw logits
(one unbounded real-valued score per vocabulary token), and
the softmax converts those scores into a probability distribution, a bias term
is added to any token whose integer token ID appears as a key in the
Tokens not in the map are left unchanged (
is then passed through softmax to produce the final sampling probabilities:
On OpenAI-compatible APIs the parameter is a JSON object mapping integer token IDs to numeric offsets, typically constrained to
makes a token's post-softmax probability negligible — effectively a
is a gentle nudge upward. The bias applies at
Because token IDs are model- and tokenizer-specific, you must look them up using the matching tokenizer (e.g.,
for OpenAI models) before constructing any bias map. A word like
might tokenize to one ID in one model and two IDs in another. Always inspect tokenization first.

In production agentic systems, the difference between a model that reliably emits structured tool-call payloads and one that occasionally replies in prose instead of JSON is the difference between a working pipeline and one requiring constant error handling. Logit bias sits at the intersection of two concerns that arise frequently in agent development:
Format enforcement on endpoints without schema constraints.

, and JSON schema validation. Older endpoints, self-hosted models, or deployments predating these features lack them. Logit bias provides a fallback mechanism to increase the probability of format-signaling tokens.

Some requirements are best handled at the vocabulary level: suppressing roleplay injection tokens, blocking toxic substrings, or preventing specific output patterns at source rather than in a post-generation filter.
For multiple-choice tasks, classification, or forced continuation, constraining the model to a small allowed set of tokens produces deterministic output without fine-tuning.
A JSON parse failure in an agent's tool-call step blocks downstream tools and can cascade into full pipeline failure. Logit bias reduces that failure mode without adding expensive retry loops.
Even developers who never ship logit bias in production gain from understanding it — it makes the logit → softmax → sample loop concrete and builds better intuitions about temperature and top-p.
Applied at inference time per request. No fine-tuning, no retraining, no model hosting changes.
Logit bias reshapes the distribution before softmax; temperature, top-p, and top-k then operate on the reshaped distribution. They do not conflict.
You can bias a single token ID while leaving every other probability unchanged. No other inference parameter provides comparable granularity.
Because bias applies at every autoregressive step, a banned token truly cannot appear anywhere in the output.
The bias addition is a sparse element-wise operation on the logit vector, performed immediately before softmax — well under 0.1 % of the softmax budget.
Unwanted tokens are prevented at source; the output never needs to be sanitized for the suppressed tokens.
A bias map built for gpt-4o-mini will not work correctly with a model using a different tokenizer. Changing models requires rebuilding every bias map.
A multi-character string may tokenize to several subword tokens. Biasing only the first provides partial effect. You must enumerate all token IDs in the string.
Bias applies at every generation step uniformly. A globally banned token cannot appear even where it would be correct — in URLs, code, or quoted strings.
Logit bias shifts single-token probabilities. It cannot enforce that a
. Schema-based constrained decoding is the right tool for structural requirements.
Strong biases (magnitude 20–100) on common tokens restructure the distribution in ways that frequently produce grammatically broken output.
with typed schemas provide far more reliable enforcement.
At each generation step, the model processes all prior tokens (using the KV cache), runs a forward pass, and projects the final hidden state through the unembedding matrix to produce a logit vector:
is the final layer's hidden state. At this point —
— the logit bias is applied element-wise for tokens with entries in the map:
then passes through softmax, and the sampler draws from the resulting distribution according to temperature and nucleus settings. The process repeats until a stop token or
Why the addition happens before softmax
If bias were added after softmax (to probabilities), you could reduce a token's probability toward zero but not eliminate it entirely. Adding before softmax means a bias of
in the denominator — astronomically small relative to unmodified logits. The token is effectively removed. Conversely, a large positive bias produces
that dominates the denominator, making the token overwhelmingly likely regardless of the model's natural preference.
, encode it with the model-specific tokenizer to get one or more integer IDs, then add each ID to the
. Always print and verify the decoded tokens before using the IDs in production.

The diagram below shows where logit bias sits in the autoregressive inference pipeline: between the raw logit tensor produced by the transformer forward pass, and the softmax step that converts logits to a probability distribution. The
map is an external input injected at this exact point.

attrs.alt: Logit bias inference-time token probability manipulation pipeline

content.text: Key observations: (1) the bias map is sparse — most token IDs have no entry, so most logits are unchanged; (2) the modified logit vector
feeds into the same softmax and sampling machinery as an unbiased generation — temperature, top-p, and top-k still apply to
normally; (3) the bias is applied independently at
Format nudge without structured output support.
or grammar-based constraints, a small positive bias on
to reduce the risk of injected prompts opening a false conversation turn.
with large positive biases on valid labels (A/B/C/D, Yes/No) for single-token classification — faster and cheaper than parsing longer responses.
A small negative bias on a token observed pathologically often in outputs from a specific prompt pattern can reduce its frequency.
In constrained generation tasks (numeric output only, code in a specific language), banning classes of off-topic tokens enforces the constraint cheaply.
on OpenAI endpoints for reliable JSON — uses constrained decoding, not probabilistic nudging.
with a defined schema when the agent must invoke a specific function.
sequences to terminate at a specific delimiter rather than banning all possible continuations.
Use fine-tuning or system prompt engineering for broad behavioral changes that apply to all outputs, not specific token-level adjustments.
The following three runnable examples show the complete workflow: inspecting tokenization, building bias maps as reusable utilities, and applying them in API calls and agentic contexts.

import tiktoken

# ─── Step 1: Inspect tokenization before building any bias map ──────────────
# Token IDs are model-specific. Always verify splits before writing IDs to code.

enc = tiktoken.encoding_for_model("gpt-4o-mini")

def show_tokenization(text: str) -> list[tuple[int, str]]:
    """Return (token_id, decoded_string) pairs for every subword in text."""
    ids = enc.encode(text)
    return [(tok_id, enc.decode([tok_id])) for tok_id in ids]

targets = ["\n", "{", "}", "```", "Human:", "true", "false", "null", "Sure", "I'll"]

print(f"{'String':<15} {'IDs':<30} {'Decoded tokens'}")
print("-" * 70)
for s in targets:
    pairs = show_tokenization(s)
    ids_str = str([p[0] for p in pairs])
    decoded = str([p[1] for p in pairs])
    print(f"{repr(s):<15} {ids_str:<30} {decoded}")

# ─── Step 2: Build a bias map from human-readable specs ─────────────────────
def bias_for_string(s: str, value: int) -> dict[int, int]:
    """
    Return logit_bias entries for ALL token IDs in the encoded string.
    Biasing only the first token of a multi-token string is a common mistake;
    this function covers all subwords.
    """
    return {tok_id: value for tok_id in enc.encode(s)}

# Mild positive nudge toward JSON object delimiters
json_open  = bias_for_string("{", value=5)
json_close = bias_for_string("}", value=3)

# Hard ban on roleplay role-injection tokens
injection_ban = {}
for s in ["\nHuman:", "\nAssistant:", "\n\nHuman:"]:
    injection_ban.update(bias_for_string(s, value=-100))

# Mild suppression of common prose starters (not hard bans)
prose_suppress = {}
for s in ["Sure", "Certainly", "Of course"]:
    prose_suppress.update(bias_for_string(s, value=-15))

# Combine: later keys win on collision; be intentional about ordering
bias_map: dict[int, int] = {}
bias_map.update(json_open)
bias_map.update(json_close)
bias_map.update(injection_ban)
bias_map.update(prose_suppress)

print("\nConstructed logit_bias map:")
for tok_id, bias_val in sorted(bias_map.items()):
    decoded = enc.decode([tok_id])
    print(f"  token_id={tok_id:6d}  bias={bias_val:+4d}  decoded={repr(decoded)}")
. A common mistake is assuming that
share the same token ID. They often do not. Always verify before writing any token ID to a bias map.

from openai import OpenAI
import tiktoken

client = OpenAI()
enc = tiktoken.encoding_for_model("gpt-4o-mini")

# ─── Utility: build bias map from human-readable spec ───────────────────────
def build_bias_map(
    ban: list[str] | None = None,
    nudge_up: list[str] | None = None,
    nudge_down: list[str] | None = None,
    nudge_strength: int = 5,
) -> dict[int, int]:
    """
    Construct a logit_bias dict from human-readable token strings.
    Encodes each string to token IDs using tiktoken.

    Parameters
    ----------
    ban          : strings to block (bias = -100)
    nudge_up     : strings to favor (bias = +nudge_strength)
    nudge_down   : strings to disfavor (bias = -nudge_strength)
    nudge_strength: magnitude for nudges (default 5; keep <= 10 for fluency)
    """
    bias: dict[int, int] = {}
    for s in (ban or []):
        for tok_id in enc.encode(s):
            bias[tok_id] = -100
    for s in (nudge_up or []):
        for tok_id in enc.encode(s):
            bias[tok_id] = nudge_strength
    for s in (nudge_down or []):
        for tok_id in enc.encode(s):
            bias[tok_id] = -nudge_strength
    return bias

# ─── Example 1: JSON nudge on a legacy endpoint ─────────────────────────────
json_bias = build_bias_map(
    nudge_up=["{", "}"],
    ban=["\nHuman:", "\nAssistant:"],
)

resp1 = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-4o-mini",
    messages=[
        {"role": "system", "content": "Respond in JSON only. No prose."},
        {"role": "user",   "content": "List two primary colors."},
    ],
    logit_bias=json_bias,
    max_tokens=60,
    temperature=0,
)
print("JSON nudge output:", resp1.choices[0].message.content)

# ─── Example 2: Forced multiple-choice (single-token answer) ─────────────────
# Strong positive bias on valid letters; max_tokens=1 locks output to one token.
choice_bias = build_bias_map(nudge_up=["A", "B", "C", "D"], nudge_strength=20)

resp2 = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-4o-mini",
    messages=[
        {"role": "system", "content": "Answer multiple-choice questions with a single letter only: A, B, C, or D."},
        {"role": "user",   "content": "Which planet is closest to the Sun?\nA) Venus  B) Mercury  C) Mars  D) Earth"},
    ],
    logit_bias=choice_bias,
    max_tokens=1,
    temperature=0,
)
print("Forced-choice answer:", resp2.choices[0].message.content)

# ─── Example 3: Repetition suppression ───────────────────────────────────────
# Identify tokens that appear pathologically often in a specific prompt pattern,
# then apply a mild negative bias to reduce their frequency.
suppress_bias = build_bias_map(
    nudge_down=["very", "extremely", "absolutely", "definitely"],
    nudge_strength=8,
)

resp3 = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-4o-mini",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Describe water's importance to life in two sentences."}],
    logit_bias=suppress_bias,
    max_tokens=80,
    temperature=0.7,
)
print("Repetition-suppressed output:", resp3.choices[0].message.content)
, not just the first. For single-character tokens like
this produces one entry, but for multi-byte strings it covers all subwords.
Phase-scoped bias in an agentic tool-call pipeline
The most effective pattern is to apply logit bias
only during the specific generation call that produces constrained output
: no bias during free-form reasoning, and a structural JSON bias during the tool-call payload step. This avoids degrading natural language quality while gaining structured output reliability.

from openai import OpenAI
import tiktoken, json
from typing import Any

client = OpenAI()
enc = tiktoken.encoding_for_model("gpt-4o-mini")

# ─── Pattern: phase-scoped logit bias for agent tool calls ──────────────────
# Apply bias ONLY during the generation call that produces a structured payload.
# Free-form reasoning and user-facing responses get no bias.

def get_tool_call_bias() -> dict[int, int]:
    """
    Bias map applied during the tool-call JSON generation phase only.
    Nudges structural JSON tokens; mildly suppresses prose-start tokens.
    """
    bias: dict[int, int] = {}
    # Small positive nudge on JSON structural characters
    for ch in ["{", "}", "[", "]", ":", ","]:
        for tok_id in enc.encode(ch):
            bias[tok_id] = 3
    # Mild suppression of tokens that indicate the model is narrating
    for phrase in ["Sure", "Certainly", "I'll", "I will", "Let me"]:
        for tok_id in enc.encode(phrase):
            bias.setdefault(tok_id, -30)  # don't override JSON nudges on collision
    return bias


TOOL_CALL_BIAS = get_tool_call_bias()  # compute once at startup


def call_tool(user_query: str, context: str = "") -> dict[str, Any]:
    """
    Generate a JSON tool-call object, with structural bias applied.
    Validates JSON on receipt; raises ValueError on parse failure.

    Returns a dict with keys 'tool' and 'args'.
    """
    system_prompt = (
        "You are a function-calling agent. When asked to perform an action, "
        "respond with a JSON object in exactly this shape: "
        '{"tool": "<tool_name>", "args": {<key>: <value>, ...}}. '
        "Available tools: search_web(query), send_email(to, subject, body), "
        "read_file(path), write_file(path, content). "
        "Respond with JSON only. No prose, no markdown."
    )

    response = client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o-mini",
        messages=[
            {"role": "system", "content": system_prompt},
            *([{"role": "user", "content": f"Context: {context}"}] if context else []),
            {"role": "user", "content": user_query},
        ],
        logit_bias=TOOL_CALL_BIAS,
        max_tokens=200,
        temperature=0,
        stop=["\n\n"],  # Belt-and-suspenders: stop at double newline
    )

    raw = response.choices[0].message.content.strip()
    try:
        return json.loads(raw)
    except json.JSONDecodeError as exc:
        raise ValueError(
            f"Tool call produced non-JSON even with bias: {raw!r}"
        ) from exc


# ─── Demo ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
queries = [
    "Search the web for the latest Python 3.13 release notes.",
    "Send an email to alice@example.com, subject 'Standup', body 'I am out today.'",
    "Read the file at /etc/config.yaml.",
]

for query in queries:
    try:
        result = call_tool(query)
        print(f"Query  : {query}")
        print(f"Tool   : {result.get('tool', '?')}")
        print(f"Args   : {json.dumps(result.get('args', {}))}")
    except ValueError as e:
        print(f"Error  : {e}")
    print()
Understanding the magnitude of the effect
To develop intuition for bias magnitudes, consider a concrete example. Suppose at some generation step the model assigns logits for the five highest-scoring tokens:
has roughly 30 % probability. Apply a nudge of
nudge took a 30 % token to near-certainty, because the softmax denominator is dominated by
even small biases can have large effects
when the natural distribution is already concentrated. Calibrate magnitudes on your specific model and prompt distribution, not by intuition alone.
A customer support automation platform deploys an agentic pipeline where a language model classifies incoming support tickets and emits a structured JSON action object — one of
— on every ticket. The self-hosted model endpoint predates
support, and occasional prose responses cause JSON parse failures that stall the pipeline.
The engineering team builds a targeted bias map: a positive nudge on
(the expected first token of the JSON action object), a mild suppression on common prose starters (
), and a hard ban on role-injection delimiters. They run a baseline evaluation on 500 sampled tickets, measuring JSON parse success rate before and after applying the bias.
The parse success rate improves from 91 % to 97 %. The remaining 3 % — the model generating multi-line JSON instead of the expected single-line object — is fixed separately by adding a
. The team documents three operational lessons: (1) logit bias solved the
— that required a stop sequence and retry loop; (3) the improvement was confirmed empirically, not assumed from intuition about bias magnitudes.
Hardcoding token IDs without model version checks
Hardcoding a token ID integer like
in application code creates a hidden dependency on the tokenizer version. When the provider updates a model, token IDs may shift. Derive IDs at startup with
so they are always correct for the deployed model.
Biasing only the first token of a multi-token string

tokenizes to three IDs, biasing only the first means the remaining tokens are still accessible. The model can still produce the full string after generating the first token through a different path. Always iterate over
Using -100 on tokens required by the task

) to produce single-line JSON will silently break any task that involves multi-line JSON, code strings, or values containing newlines. Evaluate the blast radius of any ban on a diverse eval set before deploying.
Applying static bias globally across all agent steps

An agent that applies a JSON structural bias at the session level will degrade every natural language response — reasoning steps, summaries, user-facing explanations — not just tool-call payloads. Always scope the bias to the specific sub-call that requires structured output.
Expecting logit bias to enforce schema validity
Logit bias cannot enforce that a JSON object contains a
key, that a value has the correct type, or that required fields are present. It shifts single-token probabilities; it does not implement a grammar. For schema validity, use
with a JSON schema, or post-generation validation with retry.
Treating bias effects as portable across models

may shift probabilities dramatically on one model and negligibly on another with different logit scale distributions. Benchmark every bias map on the specific model and prompt distribution you deploy against.
Inspect tokenization before every new bias map.
Print and verify decoded tokens from
before writing IDs to code. This takes two minutes and prevents hard-to-diagnose bugs.
. Larger values risk dominating the softmax. Reserve
for genuine bans of narrow token sets.
Before deploying, measure both target metric improvement (JSON parse rate) and regressions (output quality, task accuracy) on a representative sample.
Scope bias to the specific generation call.
Do not apply a structural bias globally. Apply it only to the sub-call generating the constrained output.
Store the human-readable target strings, the tokenizer model name, and the bias values alongside the derived token IDs. Raw integers without context are unmaintainable.
Rerun your evaluation suite after every model update. Even when token IDs are stable, logit scale shifts can change the effective impact of bias magnitudes.
Combine with stop sequences and retry.
Logit bias improves first-token compliance. Stop sequences and parse-validate-retry loops handle structural completeness. Use both.
Prefer first-class structured output when available.
with typed schemas, use those. Logit bias is a workaround, not a primary API.

Logit bias imposes negligible compute overhead. The operation is a sparse element-wise addition on the logit vector, performed immediately before log-softmax. For a vocabulary of 100,000 tokens and a bias map of 20 entries, the cost is 20 additions versus 100,000 floating-point operations for the softmax — under 0.1 % of inference compute.
Build bias maps once at startup.
Encode all target strings at application startup and cache the resulting
OpenAI allows up to 300 entries. Even without a cap, large maps are harder to reason about and more likely to produce unintended token interactions.
with a bias that favors the valid tokens produces single-token responses at minimum cost and maximum throughput.
vLLM, llama.cpp, and Ollama all support logit bias through their OpenAI-compatible endpoints. There is no per-request overhead at the batch scheduling level.
Dict[int, float], range [-100, 100], up to 300 entries
Identical to OpenAI; matching tokenizer IDs for same model families
OpenAI-compatible endpoint; applied in SamplingParams before sampling
JSON array of [token_id, bias] pairs in /completions endpoint
Subclass LogitsWarper; pass to model.generate() via LogitsProcessorList
No logit bias surface; use prompt engineering or response_format if available
No logit bias parameter in the public Gemini API
Required to obtain token IDs for OpenAI model families
For Hugging Face pipelines, the equivalent to logit bias is a custom
to add your bias tensor to
(the raw logit tensor) and return the modified tensor.
Model-specific IDs; cannot enforce multi-token structure
Full schema enforcement; field presence and types
Requires endpoint support; higher API surface overhead
Tool-use pattern only; not for general generation
Terminates at a delimiter; no token ID knowledge needed
Does not prevent token mid-sequence; cannot ban entirely
Can enforce any text pattern or semantic rule
Requires retry loops; adds latency on failures
Expensive; requires labeled data; non-trivial to maintain
The practical hierarchy for format enforcement in descending reliability:
(safety net). Logit bias sits in the middle — stronger than prompting, weaker than schema-based decoding.
Logit bias is not a security boundary

A sufficiently adversarial prompt can route around token-level bans through paraphrase, Unicode homoglyphs, or multi-token constructions. Logit bias suppresses specific subword tokens, not semantic concepts. Do not rely on it as the sole defense against content policy violations in user-facing systems. Use it as one layer within a defense-in-depth architecture.

) reduces but does not eliminate prompt injection risk. An attacker can use Unicode separators, alternative whitespace, or context-specific injections not relying on those exact tokens. Combine logit bias with input sanitization, sandboxed prompt construction, and output validation.
with an explicit model name, not
, to ensure the encoding matches the deployed model.
Track JSON parse success in production. A drop after a model version update signals that the bias map needs recalibration or that a first-class structured output API is now available.
Test with adversarial and edge-case inputs.
Include inputs that contain the strings you are trying to ban, multilingual inputs, and very long contexts in your evaluation set.
, and common punctuation appear in the vast majority of text. Suppressing them will systematically degrade output quality across all generation.

Think of the vocabulary as a ballot with one candidate per token. At each generation step, the model casts votes — the logits — representing how strongly it believes each token should come next given the context. Softmax converts those vote counts into vote shares that sum to 1.

Logit bias is like stuffing the ballot box for specific candidates: you add extra votes to tokens you want to win, or apply a massive deficit to tokens you want to eliminate. The other candidates' vote counts are unchanged, but their
of the total shifts because the softmax denominator now includes the biased entries.
relative differences matter, not absolute values
or below, that token dominates the softmax. But if another token scores
nudge barely changes the outcome. This is why you must understand your model's natural logit distribution for your task and prompt, not just pick bias magnitudes by intuition.
. It shifts the probability distribution without touching model weights.
The API parameter is a dict of integer token ID → numeric offset. Values near -100 ban a token; values of +1 to +8 nudge it upward.
Use the model's matching tokenizer to get IDs. Multi-token strings require all constituent IDs to be biased.
Bias applies at every autoregressive step uniformly — a banned token cannot appear anywhere in the output. Scope carefully.
Even small nudges (+5) can dominate the softmax if other tokens have similar logit values. Calibrate magnitudes empirically on your specific model and prompt.
Logit bias cannot enforce multi-token structural constraints (matched brackets, required fields). Use schema-based constrained decoding for those.
In agentic pipelines, apply bias only during structured output generation calls — not globally across the session.
) when available. Logit bias is a fallback, not a primary mechanism.
